column33: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is a type of cancer in which the bone marrow makes abnormal blood cells. AML is a cancer that starts in the bone marrow (the soft@ spongy inner part of bones) but quickly moves into the blood. It is called acute because it progresses quickly@ and myeloid because it affects a group of white blood cells called the myeloid cells@ which normally develop into various types of mature blood cells@ such as red blood cells@ white blood cells@ and platelets. In AML@ the bone marrow produces a large number of abnormal white blood cells that can't carry out their normal infection-fighting duties. These abnormal cells also crowd out the healthy blood cells.
column34: AML often presents with non-specific symptoms such as fatigue@ fever@ and weight loss. The specific symptoms depend on the type of blood cells affected. Anemia (low red blood cell count) leads to fatigue and weakness. Thrombocytopenia (low platelet count) leads to easy bruising and bleeding. Neutropenia (low neutrophil count) leads to frequent infections. Infiltration of leukemic cells into organs can cause symptoms such as bone pain@ enlarged liver or spleen@ or skin lesions.
column35: AML is caused by genetic mutations that disrupt the normal development of blood cells in the bone marrow. These mutations can be acquired during a person's lifetime or can be inherited from a parent.

column34: Symptoms of APL often develop rapidly over a period of days or weeks. The most common symptoms are related to the reduced number of normal blood cells@ including fatigue@ weakness@ fever@ and bleeding. Bleeding can manifest as nosebleeds@ bleeding gums@ easy bruising@ or heavy menstrual periods in women. Other symptoms may include bone pain@ headache@ dizziness@ shortness of breath@ and frequent infections.
column35: APL is caused by a genetic abnormality that involves a translocation between chromosomes 15 and 17. This translocation results in the fusion of the promyelocytic leukemia (PML) gene on chromosome 15 with the retinoic acid receptor alpha (RARα) gene on chromosome 17. The resulting PML-RARα fusion protein blocks the normal differentiation of promyelocytes@ leading to their accumulation in the bone marrow. The exact cause of the chromosomal translocation is unknown@ but it is not thought to be inherited.

column41: Treatment for APL typically involves a combination of all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) and arsenic trioxide (ATO). column35: The exact cause of adrenal cortex carcinoma is unknown. Adrenal cortex carcinoma forms when something goes wrong with the DNA inside adrenal gland cells. The DNA contains instructions that tell a cell what to do. Changes to the DNA instructions can cause the cell to become cancerous. The cancer cells grow and multiply rapidly. The cancer cells might break away and spread to other parts of the body. Most often@ adrenal cortex carcinoma begins with a change in the DNA that happens after birth. These changes are called somatic mutations and aren't passed to children. Less often@ adrenal cortex carcinoma is caused by an inherited change in the DNA. These changes are called germline mutations. Germline mutations can be passed to children.

column35: The exact causes of adrenocortical carcinoma aren't clear. Adrenocortical carcinoma forms when cells in the adrenal gland develop changes (mutations) in their DNA. Cancer cells can also break away and spread (metastasize) to other parts of the body.

They produce hormones that give your body instructions@ helping to regulate your blood pressure@ blood sugar@ and other important functions. Adrenocortical carcinoma is also called adrenal cancer. It can occur at any age. But it's most likely to affect children younger than 5 and adults in their 40s and 50s. When adrenocortical carcinoma is found early@ it has a chance of being cured. But if the cancer spreads to other areas of the body@ cure is less likely. Treatment may involve surgery and medicines.
column33: Adrenal cortex carcinoma is a rare and aggressive cancer of the adrenal glands@ which produce essential hormones. Symptoms can include abdominal pain@ weight loss@ nausea@ vomiting@ and fatigue. Diagnosis involves imaging tests@ hormone level assessments@ and biopsy. Treatment typically includes surgery@ chemotherapy@ and radiation therapy.
column34: Symptoms of adrenal cortex carcinoma can be varied and often nonspecific@ depending on the size of the tumor and the hormones it produces. Common symptoms include abdominal pain or fullness@ unexplained weight loss@ nausea@ vomiting@ fever@ loss of appetite@ fatigue@ muscle weakness@ and skin changes (such as darkening). Hormonal imbalances can lead to additional symptoms@ such as high blood pressure@ high blood sugar@ and virilization (development of male characteristics in females) or feminization (development of female characteristics in males).
column35: The exact cause of adrenal cortex carcinoma is not fully understood@ but it is thought to involve a combination of genetic and environmental factors.
